data.title: Calfrac Well Services Slashes Workforce, Reduces North America Footprint on Global Uncertainty
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Calfrac Well Services has reduced its footprint in North America in response to softening oil and gas activity after second-quarter results.

The Calgary, Alberta-based oilfield services cited on Friday oil prices that are affected by geopolitical uncertainty and tensions, as well as oil supply increases, and shifting U.S. trade and tariff policies.

Calfrac said these have resulted in mixed signals regarding the outlook for the global economy, which continues to weigh on near-term crude oil prices and its own sentiment moving forward.

"Although these events have not yet triggered a meaningful North American supply response, oil-weighted completion activity is expected to be lower year-over-year," the company said.

As a result, Calfrac chose to reduce its operating footprint to ten fleets in North America to better align with anticipated activity levels for the remainder of the year, and also took laid off staff at the divisional, district and corporate levels in North America.

While the company didn't disclose details of the layoffs, it recorded 5.4 million Canadian dollars ($3.9 million) of restructuring costs.

Revenue in the second quarter fell by 6% to C$402.3 million, dragging net income down to C$15.3 million from C$24.5 million a year earlier. On a per-share basis, this was a decline to C$0.18 from C$0.29.

The decline in revenue was primarily from lower activity and pricing in North America, offsetting revenue in Argentina, which was the bright spot of the quarter.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Street View: Optimistic about launch of Gilead's twice-yearly HIV prevention shot
data.description:
** Gilead Sciences on Thursday reported flat second-quarter earnings on slightly higher revenue and raised its full-year financial outlook, citing better-than-expected sales of HIV drugs

** The drugmaker did not disclose sales of Yeztugo, a twice-yearly HIV prevention drug approved by U.S. regulators in June, but CEO Daniel O'Day told Reuters the company is very happy with the launch so far

** Shares up 3.38% to $114.01 in premarket trading

PrEP MARKET RIPE FOR YEZTUGO LAUNCH SUCCESS

** BMO Capital Markets ('Outperform', PT: $130) says co's quarterly results provided 'a breath of fresh air with a clean beat and raise', HIV business was firing on all cylinders

** J.P. Morgan ('Overweight', PT: $135) adds company's comments on Yeztugo launch in PrEP was confident, which points to encouraging launch trends thus far

** Bernstein ('Outperform', PT: $120) notes the PrEP market ripe for Yeztugo launch success

** Oppenheimer ('Outperform', PT: $128) says enthusiasm around PrEP couldn't come at a better time, with favorable Supreme Court ruling requiring health insurers to cover certain recommended preventive services cost-free

data.title: RealReal Stock Surges on Boosted Guidance as 2Q Results Beat Estimates
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Shares of The RealReal climbed after the company boosted its full-year guidance as its second-quarter results beat Wall Street's expectations in the latest quarter.

The stock rose 36% to $7.50 on Friday in premarket trading. Shares are down 50% this year through Thursday's close.

The luxury-resale marketplace said late Thursday it now expects full-year revenue of $667 million to $674 million, up from its prior guidance of $645 million to $660 million. Analysts polled by FactSet are expecting $663.3 million.

It now forecasts adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $29 million to $32 million, compared with its previous view of $20 million to $30 million.

The updated guidance came as the San Francisco company logged improved second-quarter results, helped by a rise in luxury resale as consumers embraced a circular economy, according to Chief Executive Rati Levesque.

The RealReal expects to sustain growth and improve profitability going forward given efficiency gains from artificial intelligence and automation, Levesque said.

For the second quarter, the company posted a loss of $11.4 million, or 13 cents a share, compared with a loss of $16.7 million, or 20 cents a share, a year earlier.

Its adjusted loss per share was 6 cents. Analysts modeled an adjusted loss of 9 cents per share.

Revenue rose 14% to $165.2 million, topping the $159.7 million expected by analysts.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Sensex Ends at 3-Month Low
data.description:
India's BSE Sensex deepened losses to close nearly 1% down at 79,857.8 on Friday, a level last seen in May 9th, amid tariff-related concerns, unimpressive earnings and heavy foreign capital outflows.

Trump’s tariff move—raising duties to a steep 50% from August 27 while firmly ruling out trade talks—has rattled investors and raised fresh concerns over the future of US-India relations.

Additionally, Moody's warned that the steeper tariffs could severely curtail India’s ambitions to develop its manufacturing sector.

About 25 out of 30 stocks ended in negative terrain, led by Bharti Airtel, Tata Motors, M&M, Kotak Mahindra Bank, and Reliance Industries.

On the other hand, NTPC, Titan, Trent, ITC, and Bajaj FinServ were only five gains.

data.title: Trade Desk Stock Crashes 30% After Earnings. What's Causing the Selloff. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Elsa Ohlen

Trade Desk investors were fleeing en masse after the ad-tech company reported second-quarter earnings and guidance more or less in line with expectations but said its revenue growth is expected to slow down in the third quarter.

Possibly spooked by signs of a slowdown and the sudden departure of Chief Financial Officer Laura Schenkein, investors appear to have reacted harshly.

Shares were down 30% to $61.61 in premarket trading Friday. The move is set to more than wipe out the stock's 24% gain over the past three months.

The company's guide for revenue of at least $717 million in the current quarter was largely in line with expectations according to FactSet. However, Jefferies analysts led by James Heaney notes that it implies a growth rate of 14% — a slowdown from the 19% growth observed in the previous quarter.

That likely creates concerns over long-term growth, Heaney said in a late Thursday research note. The analyst has a Buy rating on the stock with a price target of $100.

As for the cause of the slowdown, there are several potential culprits. Amazon could be eating into Trade Desk's market share, however, management says that isn't the case, Heaney noted. Macro-related events are also playing their part, causing its customer-base of large companies to be cautious on spending.

Finally, there's an organizational revamp underway as Trade Desk is transforming from an emerging company to one with quarterly sales in the multibillion-dollar range. It was added to the S&P 500 in July this year.

Alongside second-quarter earnings released after the closing bell Thursday, the company announced that CFO Laura Schenkein will be replaced by Alex Kayyal — a board member who spent nearly a decade at Salesforce in executive roles — on Aug. 21. The change in executive leadership comes as the company's Chief Operating Officer and Chief Revenue Officer have also been replaced within the last 24 months.

As for the earnings themselves, they appeared solid. Revenue came in at $694 million, ahead of expectations. Adjusted earnings per share were in line with expectations at 41 cents.

"For now investors will continue to debate what's driving the slowdown and when the headwinds will lift," Heaney said.

Write to Elsa Ohlen at elsa.ohlen@barrons.com
